steps.description: Prepare xylene/ferrocene solution with 10% concentration of ferrocene.

Inject xylene/ferrocene solution into the reaction zone of a quartz reactor for high-temperature pyrolysis. Ferrocene decomposes to produce Fe phase nanoparticles (including Fe3C) which serve as catalyst for CNT growth and intercalate into the CNT arrays.

equipment.name: quartz reactor
equipment.settings: floating catalyst CVD, high-temperature pyrolysis at 875°C

synthesis.notes: Fe3C (iron carbide, orthorhombic structure, space group Pbnm) is the dominant Fe phase (~90%) produced as nanoparticles intercalated within the CNT arrays during the floating catalyst CVD synthesis. The XRD pattern also shows Fe5C2 and the Mössbauer spectrum identifies fcc γ-Fe, bcc α-Fe, and Fe3C phases. The Fe phase nanoparticles are formed as a result of ferrocene decomposition and serve as catalysts for CNT growth while simultaneously intercalating into the CNT array.

synthesis.target_compound: Fe/CNT nanostructures (Fe phase nanosphere/conical CNT bundle nanostructures)

starting_materials.name: Vertically aligned carbon nanotube array intercalated with Fe phase nanoparticles

steps.description: CNT arrays intercalated with Fe phase nanoparticles were synthesized on Si substrates by floating catalyst CVD via high-temperature pyrolysis of xylene/ferrocene solution injected into the reaction zone of a quartz reactor. Ferrocene concentration was 10%, temperature was 875°C, and process duration was 30 s.

FSL irradiation was performed in ambient atmosphere using a laser scanning microscope. The femtosecond titanium-sapphire laser delivered 100 fs pulses at 790 nm wavelength with 82 MHz repetition rate. Single pulse energy was 15 nJ, attenuated to 6.25 nJ at the focal plane. The laser beam was focused by a 40x/0.75 objective to a 1.2 μm diameter spot. The CNT array was scanned line-by-line at 512 lines per scan resolution at ~145 mm/s scan speed. Scan area varied from 230×230 μm to 30×30 μm. 3D scanning was performed with 1 μm Z-axis steps.

synthesis.notes: The Fe/CNT nanostructures are formed by FSL ablation of the topmost layer of the CNT array, creating a cavity ~10 μm deep. Fe phase nanospheres (40–680 nm diameter) form at the tips of conical CNT bundles, and smaller nanospheres (10–30 nm) bead the sides of the bundles. Some larger nanospheres are encapsulated in carbon shells which sometimes contain CNTs. The mechanism involves ablation of C and Fe, plasma formation, Fe condensation into spherical clusters, and re-deposition onto CNT cone tips. The CNT array precursor was synthesized by floating catalyst CVD using 10 wt% ferrocene in xylene at 875°C for 30 s.

synthesis.notes: The Fe/C nanostructures are formed through femtosecond laser ablation of vertically aligned CNT arrays intercalated with Fe phase nanoparticles. The laser irradiation transforms the topmost layer of CNT array into iron phase nanospheres (40 to 680 nm in diameter) located at the tip of CNT bundles of conical shape. Smaller nanospheres (10 to 30 nm) are beaded at the sides of these bundles. Some larger nanospheres are encapsulated into carbon shells. The mechanism involves ablation of both C and Fe materials, formation of localized weak plasma, rapid condensation and aggregation of Fe into spherical clusters, and subsequent cooling with possible graphitic shell formation.
